perturbation.original_text: BATTELLE and its representative(s) shall maintain the identified Proprietary Information in confidence for a period of three (3) years from the effective date of this Agreement. During this period, BATTELLE shall not divulge such information to any third party or use such information for any purpose other than review and evaluation without the prior written consent of ADC. BATTELLE shall treat such information with the same degree of care as it accords to its own proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: BATTELLE and its representative(s) should endeavor to maintain the identified Proprietary Information in confidence for a period of three (3) years from the effective date of this Agreement. During this period, BATTELLE will try not to divulge such information to any third party or use such information for any purpose other than review and evaluation without the expressed consent of ADC. BATTELLE shall endeavor to treat such information with a similar degree of care as it accords to its own proprietary information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain' to 'should endeavor to maintain', 'shall not divulge' to 'will try not to divulge', 'prior written consent' to 'expressed consent', and 'same degree of care' to 'similar degree of care' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, making the requirements less strict and enforceable. 'Expressed consent' could be interpreted more loosely than 'prior written consent'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts RCW 19.108.010, which protects trade secrets by outlining civil remedies for misappropriation. By weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality, the modified text makes it more difficult for ADC to prove misappropriation of trade secrets if BATTELLE fails to adequately protect the information. 'Endeavor to maintain' and 'try not to divulge' are not strong enough to provide a legal protection that would prevent potential misappropriation of a trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject

perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, which creates a legal contradiction. The original text uses 'shall maintain,' 'shall not divulge,' and 'same degree of care,' which impose clear obligations. The changed text uses 'should endeavor to maintain,' 'will try not to divulge,' and 'similar degree of care,' which are less strict and therefore make it more difficult to prove misappropriation if BATTELLE fails to protect the information. This directly contradicts the intention of RCW 19.108.010, as it relates to maintaining secrecy in trade secret protection, since it weakens the required standard of care. The law snippets define 'misappropriation' in terms of 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy' where 'improper means' are used. The modified contract language makes it harder to prove such a breach, thus contradicting the law's intent to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information disclosed hereunder shall remain the property of ADC. No license under any patent, copyright, trademark or trade secret is granted or implied.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information disclosed hereunder generally remains the property of ADC. Licenses under any patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret might be granted or implied.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'generally' before 'remains the property' introduces ambiguity as to whether the proprietary information always remains the property of ADC. Also, changing 'No license...is granted or implied' to 'Licenses...might be granted or implied' creates uncertainty about whether licenses are or are not being granted, which could lead to disputes over intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The alteration suggests that licenses might be implied or granted, which conflicts with the explicit protection and ownership rights afforded under Washington's Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The ambiguity around ownership could undermine ADC's ability to protect its trade secrets under RCW 19.108.010, as it weakens their claim to exclusive ownership and control. By suggesting licenses 'might' be granted, the contract creates potential for unauthorized use and disclosure, thereby contradicting the principles of trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The altered contract language introduces ambiguity that undermines the original intent to protect proprietary information, potentially conflicting with the Uniform Trade Secrets Act as codified in RCW 19.108.010. By changing "shall remain the property of ADC" to "generally remains the property of ADC", the contract creates uncertainty about ownership. Furthermore, changing "No license...is granted or implied" to "Licenses...might be granted or implied" directly opposes the initial restriction on licenses. This shift could allow for unauthorized use and disclosure of trade secrets, contradicting the principles of trade secret protection outlined in RCW 19.108.010, which focuses on preventing 'misappropriation' through unauthorized 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Washington and any action brought to enforce any provision or obligation hereunder shall be brought in a court of competent jurisdiction in the State of Washington. The prevailing party in any such proceeding shall be entitled to receive from the other party all reasonable attorneys' fees incurred by such prevailing party and all costs reasonably incurred in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally governed by the laws of the State of Washington, and any action brought to enforce any provision or obligation hereunder should ideally be brought in a court within Washington. The prevailing party in any such proceeding may be eligible to receive attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be governed' to 'shall be generally governed,' 'shall be brought' to 'should ideally be brought', and 'shall be entitled to receive' to 'may be eligible to receive' weakens the governing law and enforcement provisions, creating ambiguity. The added ambiguity around where the action is brought could mean that the case would happen outside of Washington, even though the agreement specifies Washington law. The prevailing party 'may' receive fees and costs, instead of 'shall', which introduces doubt.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the principles of contract certainty within the Washington Revised Code (RCW) Title 2, which governs the structure and jurisdiction of courts in Washington. By stating the agreement is 'generally' governed by Washington law and actions 'should ideally' be brought in Washington courts, it creates uncertainty and potentially allows for litigation to occur outside of Washington, undermining the predictability and enforceability of the contract. RCW 2.04.010 establishes the superior court as having jurisdiction, and undermining this through 'ideal' locations creates ambiguity and potential conflict.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 2.04.010: Jurisdiction. Print RCWs > Title 2 > Chapter 2.04 > Section 2.04.010 Beginning of Chapter << 2.04.010 >> 2.04.020 PDF RCW 2.04.010 Jurisdiction. The supreme court shall have original jurisdiction in habeas corpus and quo warranto and mandamus as to all state officers, and appellate jurisdiction in all actions and proceedings excepting that its appellate jurisdiction shall not extend to civil actions at law for the recovery of money or personal property when the original amount in controversy or the value of the property does not exceed the sum of two hundred dollars, unless the action involves the legality of a tax, impost, assessment, toll, municipal fine, or the validity of a statute. The supreme court shall also have power to issue writs of mandamus, review, prohibition, habeas corpus, certiorari, and all other writs necessary and proper to the complete exercise of its appellate and revisory jurisdiction. Each of the judges shall have power to issue writs of habeas corpus to any part of the state, upon petition by or on behalf of any person held in actual custody, and may make such writs returnable before himself or herself, or before the supreme court, or before any superior court of the state, or any judge thereof. [ 2011 c 336 s 8 ; 1890 p 322 s 6 ; RRS s 1.] NOTES: Rules of court: Cf. RAP 4.2, 4.3, 18.22; Titles 2 and 16 RAP. Jurisdiction of supreme court: State Constitution Art. 4 s 4.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the governing law and jurisdiction, which contradicts the principle of contract certainty. The original text's 'shall be governed' and 'shall be brought' are replaced with the weaker 'generally governed' and 'should ideally be brought,' allowing for interpretations that deviate from Washington law and courts. While Snippet 1 (RCW 2.04.010) focuses on the Supreme Court's jurisdiction, the Law Explanation correctly identifies that ambiguities undermining the predictability of the contract create potential conflicts. The change from 'shall be entitled to receive' to 'may be eligible to receive' regarding attorney's fees further weakens the enforceability of the contract, increasing ambiguity. This aligns with the definition of an 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction,' where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: During this period, BATTELLE shall not divulge such information to any third party or use such information for any purpose other than review and evaluation without the prior written consent of ADC.
perturbation.changed_text: During this period, BATTELLE will attempt to not divulge such information to unaffiliated third parties or use such information for commercial purposes other than preliminary review without expressed consent from ADC representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not divulge' to 'will attempt to not divulge' significantly weakens the obligation to protect the proprietary information. Narrowing 'third party' to 'unaffiliated third parties' creates a loophole where information can be divulged to affiliated third parties. Changing 'any purpose other than review and evaluation' to 'commercial purposes other than preliminary review' opens the door for use in other non-commercial purposes. Replacing 'prior written consent of ADC' to 'expressed consent from ADC representatives' introduces ambiguity in who can provide consent and if it requires a written form.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the protection of trade secrets, making it easier for BATTELLE to potentially misappropriate ADC's proprietary information without clear legal repercussions. By limiting the restriction to 'unaffiliated' third parties and 'commercial' purposes, the revised text creates loopholes that undermine the comprehensive protection intended by the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (RCW 19.108.010). This act aims to prevent the unauthorized use and disclosure of trade secrets, but the changes in the modified text provide avenues for such misuse. Additionally, 'expressed consent from ADC representatives' is less legally sound than 'prior written consent of ADC', which also undermines protection.

Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, tru
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information, potentially conflicting with the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Specifically, changing 'shall not divulge' to 'will attempt to not divulge' weakens the commitment. Limiting the restriction to 'unaffiliated third parties' creates a loophole. Changing 'any purpose other than review and evaluation' to 'commercial purposes other than preliminary review' allows use in other non-commercial contexts. 'Expressed consent from ADC representatives' is less legally sound than 'prior written consent of ADC'. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, RCW 19.108.010, 'Misappropriation' includes 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. The original text provided a higher standard of protection, while the changes create ambiguities making it easier to argue there was 'implied consent' or that the disclosure didn't constitute misappropriation. These changes undermine the original intent and introduce loopholes that could lead to the unauthorized use or disclosure of trade secrets, as the law seeks to prevent.
